text: the domain name space consists of a tree data structure. each node or leaf in the tree has a label and zero or more resource records (rr), which hold information associated with the domain name. the domain name itself consists of the label, concatenated with the name of its parent node on the right, separated by a dot.

the data link layer is responsible for reliable communication across a physical link between devices. one of its primary functions is framing, where raw bits received from the physical layer are organized into frames. frames contain headers and trailers that include addressing fields and error-checking codes. error detection and correction mechanisms help ensure that frames affected by noise or interference can be identified and retransmitted if necessary. flow control regulates the rate of data transmission between sender and receiver to prevent buffer overflow. mac addressing uniquely identifies each device on the network. switching concepts allow switches to examine mac addresses and forward frames efficiently.

in networking architectures, the data link layer ensures reliable frame transmission across physical links. framing groups raw bits into frames containing headers and trailers. error detection and correction techniques protect transmitted data from corruption. flow control regulates communication speed between devices. mac addressing uniquely identifies network interfaces. switching concepts allow switches to forward frames efficiently.

economic incentives: cybercrime has become a lucrative industry. ransomware attacks are projected to cost victims $57 billion this year, according to cybersecurity ventures. this figure is expected to grow dramatically to $275 billion by 2031. such enormous financial impacts create powerful incentives for threat actors to continuously refine their attack methods.

the http protocol is the web�s native language, the one spoken by web servers. http stands for hypertext transfer protocol. we will examine it in more detail later in this section, with a particular focus on https, the secure version of this protocol, which is now the predominant protocol used to serve objects on the web today.

the data link layer supports reliable frame delivery across physical connections. framing structures raw data into frames with defined boundaries. error detection and correction mechanisms ensure that transmission errors are detected. flow control synchronizes the transmission rate between communicating devices. mac addressing provides unique identification for devices in a network. switching concepts enable switches to forward frames selectively to reduce network congestion.

network virtualization allows multiple logical networks to operate on the same physical infrastructure. technologies such as vxlan and nvgre encapsulate network layer packets within other packets to create virtual overlays. this approach is widely used in cloud computing and data centers to isolate tenants and provide scalable networking services.

before looking at the services provided by computer networks, it is useful to agree on some terminology that is widely used in networking literature. first of all, computer networks are often classified in function of the geographical area that they cover � lan :a local area network typically interconnects hosts that are up to a few or maybe a few tens of kilometers apart. � man :a metropolitan area network typically interconnects devices that are up to a few hundred kilometers apart � wan :a wide area network interconnect hosts that can be located anywhere on earth 2 another classification of computer networks is based on their physical topology. in the following figures, physical links are represented as lines while boxes show computers or other types of networking equipment. computer networks are used to allow several hosts to exchange information between themselves. to allow any host to send messages to any other host in the network, the easiest solution is to organize them as a full-mesh, with a direct and dedicated link between each pair of hosts. such a physical topology is sometimes used, especially when high performance and high redundancy is required for a small number of hosts. however, it has two major drawbacks : � for a network containing n hosts, each host must have n-1 physical interfaces. in practice, the number of physical interfaces on a node will limit the size of a full-mesh network that can be built � for a network containing n hosts, n� (n?1) 2 links are required. this is possible when there are a few nodes in the same room, but rarely when they are located several kilometers apart an important aspect to understand before studying computer networks is the difference between a service and a protocol. in computer networks, the notion of service is more formally defined in [x200] . it can be better understood by considering a computer network, whatever its size or complexity, as a black box that provides a service to users , as shown in the figure below. these users could be human users or processes running on a computer system. many users can be attached to the same service provider. through this provider, each user must be able to exchange messages with any other user. to be able to deliver these messages, the service provider must be able to unambiguously identify each user. in computer networks, each user is identified by a unique address, we will discuss later how these addresses are built and used. at this point, and when considering unicast transmission, the main characteristic of these addresses is that they are unique. two different users attached to the network cannot use the same address.

in computer network design, the network layer ensures that packets are delivered from the source network to the destination network through multiple intermediate routers. logical addressing is achieved through internet protocol version 4 and internet protocol version 6. ipv4 addresses are limited, which is why subnetting techniques were introduced to efficiently allocate address ranges and reduce network congestion. subnetting also helps in organizing networks into hierarchical structures, improving routing performance. routing algorithms analyze network topology and calculate the best route between nodes. for smaller networks, protocols like routing information protocol may be used, while enterprise networks prefer open shortest path first. on the global internet, border gateway protocol is widely used to exchange routing information between autonomous systems.

layered architecture simplifies network design by dividing communication into multiple layers. each layer performs a specific function, interacts with adjacent layers, and hides its internal processes, making networks easier to manage and troubleshoot. osi model the osi (open systems interconnection) model is a conceptual framework standardizing communication across networks. it has seven layers, each with a unique function:

the data link layer manages communication across a single physical link. framing groups transmitted bits into frames. error detection and correction methods detect corrupted frames and maintain data integrity. flow control prevents congestion in communication channels. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices in a lan. switching concepts allow frames to be forwarded efficiently between network devices.

telnet is a client-server application protocol that provides access to virtual terminals of remote systems on local area networks or the internet.it is a protocol for bidirectional 8-bit communications. its main goal was to connect terminal devices and terminal-oriented processes. the name "telnet" refers to two things: a protocol specifying how two parties are to communicate and a software application that implements the protocol as a service.user data is interspersed in-band with telnet control information in an 8-bit byte oriented data connection over the transmission control protocol (tcp). telnet transmits all information including usernames and passwords in plaintext so it is not recommended for security-sensitive applications such as remote management of routers.telnet's use for this purpose has waned significantly in favor of ssh. some extensions to telnet which would provide encryption have been proposed.

physical layer protocols these standards define the speed, distance, and pin configurations for serial communication. protocol : rs-232 , rs-449 max data rate: 20 kbps , 2 mbps max distance: 10 feet, 60 feet

the transport layer is responsible for providing reliable communication between processes running on different hosts. it operates above the network layer and ensures that data is delivered from one application to another correctly. two main protocols operate at this layer: transmission control protocol (tcp) and user datagram protocol (udp). tcp provides reliable, connection-oriented communication, while udp provides faster but unreliable communication. flow control is an important mechanism used to prevent a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. congestion control is also implemented mainly in tcp to manage network traffic and prevent congestion collapse. port numbers are used to identify specific applications or services running on a host, allowing multiple programs to use network services simultaneously.

within the osi networking architecture, the data link layer ensures structured communication across physical network links. framing divides raw bit streams into frames with defined boundaries so that receiving devices can interpret data correctly. error detection and correction mechanisms such as parity checks or cyclic redundancy checks verify the accuracy of transmitted frames. flow control regulates transmission speed between sender and receiver to maintain network stability. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices within a lan environment. switching concepts allow switches to maintain mac address tables and efficiently deliver frames to their intended destinations.
presentation layer the presentation layer translates data into a format that can be understood by different systems. it also performs encryption and compression to improve security and efficiency.

data flow communication between two devices can be simplex, half-duplex, or full-duplex as shown in figure. simplex in simplex mode, the communication is unidirectional, as on a one way street. only one of the two devices on a link can transmit; the other can only receive (figure a). keyboards and traditional monitors are examples of simplex devices. half-duplex in half-duplex mode, each station can both transmit and receive, but not at the same time. when one device is sending, the other can only receive, and vice versa (figure b). walkie-talkies and cb (citizens band) radios are both half duplex systems. full-duplex in full-duplex, both stations can transmit and receive simultaneously (figure c). one common example of full-duplex communication is the telephone network. when two people are communicating by a telephone line, both can talk and listen at the same time. the full-duplex mode is used when communication in both directions is required all the time.

tcp/ip model the tcp/ip model is the networking framework used by the internet and most modern computer networks. it was developed as part of research funded by the united states department of defense to support reliable communication across interconnected networks. unlike the osi model, which is mainly theoretical, the tcp/ip model is a practical model that is widely implemented in real-world networking systems. the tcp/ip model consists of four layers: network interface layer, internet layer, transport layer, and application layer. the network interface layer is responsible for handling the physical transmission of data and interaction with network hardware. the internet layer manages logical addressing and routing of data packets across networks using protocols such as internet protocol (ip). the transport layer provides end-to-end communication and ensures reliable data delivery through protocols such as transmission control protocol (tcp) and user datagram protocol (udp). the application layer includes protocols that support user applications such as hypertext transfer protocol (http), file transfer protocol (ftp), and simple mail transfer protocol (smtp). the tcp/ip model is essential because it provides a standardized method for devices on different networks to communicate. it enables the internet to function as a global network connecting millions of devices worldwide.

a vpn (virtual private network) is a technology that creates a secure and encrypted connection between a user�s device and a remote network over the internet. it is mainly used to protect online privacy, secure data transmission, and allow safe access to private networks from remote locations. a vpn acts as a secure tunnel through which data travels, preventing unauthorized users from viewing or intercepting the information. when a user connects to a vpn, their internet traffic is encrypted and routed through a vpn server. this process hides the user�s real ip address and replaces it with the ip address of the vpn server. as a result, websites and online services see the vpn server�s location instead of the user�s actual location. this provides anonymity and helps protect against tracking and surveillance.

the physical layer is responsible for transmitting raw binary data across communication channels. it defines hardware specifications such as cables, connectors, signal voltage levels, and transmission speeds. this layer converts digital data into electrical, optical, or radio signals that can travel through communication media.

network layer the network layer is responsible for logical addressing and routing. it determines the best path for data packets to travel from the source device to the destination device across multiple networks. protocols such as ip (internet protocol) operate at this layer.

a network gateway is a node in a computer network that provides a key stopping point for data that is traveling to or from other networks. thanks to gateways, we are able to communicate online by sending data back and forth. without a gateway, the internet would not be of any use to us! additionally, many of the pieces of hardware and software that we use every day would be unusable and irrelevant.

email security: email security protects employees from cyber threats and social engineering attacks, including phishing, spear-phishing, and other email-based tactics. it works by examining inbound email to detect potential risk factors. for instance, it can identify communications that contain malware, suspicious links, questionable content and imagery, and other abnormalities � blocking the email from reaching the user�s inbox.

network models provide a structured framework for understanding how communication occurs between devices. they break down the complex process into layers, each with distinct responsibilities. osi model (open systems interconnection) � 7 layers: physical layer: transfers raw bits over a physical medium (cables, fiber, wireless). data link layer: packages bits into frames, manages error detection, and controls media access. network layer: handles logical addressing and routing; determines the path data takes across networks. transport layer: ensures reliable end-to-end communication, error recovery, and sequencing (tcp for reliable, udp for fast connectionless transport). session layer: manages sessions between applications (setup, maintenance, termination). presentation layer: formats data, manages encryption, compression, and translation. application layer: interfaces with applications; includes protocols like http, ftp, smtp, dns.

file transfer protocol (ftp) allows hosts to transfer data among dissimilar hosts, as well as files between two foreign hosts indirectly. ftp provides for such tasks as listing remote directories, changing the current remote directory, creating and removing remote directories, and transferring multiple files in a single request. ftp keeps the transport secure by passing user and account passwords to the foreign host. although ftp is designed primarily to be used by applications, it also allows interactive user-oriented sessions. ftp uses reliable stream delivery (tcp/ip) to send the files and uses a telnet connection to transfer commands and replies. ftp also understands several basic file formats including netascii, image, and local 8. tcp/ip implements ftp in the ftp user command and the ftpd server command and does not provide an applications programming interface (api) to this protocol.

lan edge: combining switching and wi-fi access points (ap), lan edge integrates connectivity and security, allowing devices connected to the internal network. switching ensures adequate security at the network edge by facilitating traffic filtering and access control at the port level, allowing administrators to enforce policies on granular network segments. and wireless access points implement encryption protocols and authentication mechanisms, protecting data in transit. they also support access control lists (acls), restricting unauthorized devices from connecting to the network.

osi (open systems interconnection) model the osi model is a conceptual framework used to understand and describe how different networking protocols and devices communicate with each other. definition: osi stands for open systems interconnection. origin: it was created by the international standards organization (iso). purpose: it serves as a reference model to explain how various networking technologies interact and work together. non-mandatory: it is a reference model, not a strict standard that every protocol must follow, though most modern protocols are mapped to it. functionality: each of the seven layers has specific, dedicated functions. interaction: all layers work together in a specific sequence to move data across a network. the seven layers of osi to remember the order of the layers, you can use the following mnemonics: application (layer 7) presentation (layer 6) session (layer 5) transport (layer 4) network (layer 3) data link (layer 2) physical (layer 1)

a cloud storage gateway is a network appliance or server which translates cloud storage apis such as soap or rest to block-based storage protocols such as iscsi, fiber channel or file-based interfaces such as nfs or cifs. cloud storage gateways enable companies to integrate private cloud storage into applications without moving the applications into a public cloud, thereby simplifying data protection.

a network protocol is a set of rules and procedures that define how data is transmitted and received between devices in a network. protocols ensure that communication between devices happens correctly, efficiently, and securely. without protocols, devices connected in a network would not be able to understand each other�s messages. tcp (transmission control protocol) is a reliable transport protocol that establishes a connection between sender and receiver before transmitting data. it ensures that data packets arrive in the correct order and without errors. ip (internet protocol) is responsible for addressing and routing data packets so they can travel across different networks and reach the correct destination. http (hypertext transfer protocol) is used for communication between web browsers and web servers. it allows users to access web pages and resources on the internet. ftp (file transfer protocol) is used for transferring files between computers over a network. dns (domain name system) translates human-readable domain names into ip addresses so that computers can locate servers on the internet.

osi reference model the osi (open systems interconnection) model consists of seven layers, each responsible for a specific part of the communication process. these layers are: physical layer � responsible for transmitting raw bits over the communication medium. data link layer � ensures reliable transmission of frames and handles error detection. network layer � manages routing and logical addressing. transport layer � provides end-to-end communication and error recovery. session layer � establishes and manages communication sessions. presentation layer � handles data translation, encryption, and compression. application layer � provides network services directly to applications. each layer communicates only with its adjacent layers, which helps simplify network design and allows different technologies to operate together.

next-generation routing technologies are being developed to improve performance, automation, and resilience in modern networks. these technologies integrate advanced routing algorithms, programmable network devices, and real-time telemetry to manage traffic efficiently. innovations such as segment routing, intent-based networking, and ai-assisted routing optimization allow networks to adapt dynamically to changing conditions. as network traffic continues to increase due to cloud computing and multimedia applications, these next-generation technologies will play a crucial role in maintaining reliable and scalable internet connectivity.

the data link layer provides link-level communication services in computer networks. framing structures transmitted bits into frames with addressing fields. error detection and correction mechanisms maintain communication reliability. flow control regulates transmission speeds between sender and receiver. mac addressing uniquely identifies network interfaces. switching concepts enable switches to deliver frames effectively.

intent-based networking allows administrators to define high-level policies describing how the network should behave. software controllers translate these policies into routing configurations and automatically adjust network behavior to meet the desired intent. this approach simplifies management and reduces configuration errors in large networks.

physical layer this layer is the lowest layer in the osi model. it helps in the transmission of data between two machines that are communicating through a physical medium, which can be optical fibers ,copper wire or wireless etc. the following are the main functions of the physical layer: hardware specification: the details of the physical cables, network interface cards, wireless radios, etc are a part of this layer. encoding and signaling: how are the bits encoded in the medium is also decided by this layer. for example, on the copper wire medium, we can use different voltage levels for a certain time interval to represent '0' and '1'. we may use +5mv for 1nsec to represent '1' and -5mv for 1nsec to represent '0'. all the issues of modulation is dealt with in this layer. eg, we may use binary phase shift keying for the representation of '1' and '0' rather than using different voltage levels if we have to transfer in rf waves. binary phase shift keying data transmission and reception: the transfer of each bit of data is the responsibility of this layer. this layer assures the transmission of each bit with a high probability. the transmission of the bits is not completely reliable as their is no error correction in this layer. topology and network design: the network design is the integral part of the physical layer. which part of the network is the router going to be placed, where the switches will be used, where we will put the hubs, how many machines is each switch going to handle, what server is going to be placed where, and many such concerns are to be taken care of by the physical layer. the various kinds of topologies that we decide to use may be ring, bus, star or a hybrid of these topologies depending on our requirements.

firewalls a firewall is inserted between the premises network and the internet to establish a controlled link and to erect an outer security wall or perimeter, forming a single choke point where security and audit can be imposed. a firewall: defines a single choke point that keeps unauthorized users out of the protected network, prohibits potentially vulnerable services from entering or leaving the network, and provides protection from various kinds of ip spoofing and routing attacks. provides a location for monitoring security-related events. is a convenient platform for several internet functions that are not security related, such as nat and internet usage audits or logs. a firewall can serve as the platform for ipsec to implement virtual private networks.

internet the internet is a system of linked networks that are worldwide in scope and facilitate data communication services such as remote login, file transfer, electronic mail, the world wide web and newsgroups. with the meteoric rise in demand for connectivity, the internet has become a communications highway for millions of users. the internet was initially restricted to military and academic institutions, but now it is a full-fledged conduit for any and all forms of information and commerce. internet websites now provide personal, educational, political and economic resources to every corner of the planet.

telnet provides communication facilities between two hosts using the cli. telnet is used for communication through the command line interface between remote device(s) or server(s). it stands for teletype network & configures elements of networking hardware the two hosts can communicate over the telnet user interface through two means: line-by-line or character-by-character basis.

dhcp is an application layer protocol used to automatically assign ip addresses and other network configuration settings (like subnet mask, default gateway, dns servers) to devices on a network. instead of manually configuring each device with network settings, dhcp enables a central server to assign and manage them dynamically. this makes networking faster, easier, and less error-prone, especially in environments with many devices (like homes, offices, or schools).

many network attacks start with the interception of network traffic. for this attack ingredient, we assume that the attacker has a presence in the victim�s network. for instance, the attacker brings a laptop in range of the victim�s wifi network, or obtains access to a pc in the wired network. sniffing on a broadcast medium, such as wifi or the original ethernet implementation is easy: you just tune into the channel at a convenient location, and listen for the bits come thundering by. to do so, attackers set their network interfaces in promiscuous mode, to make it accept all packets on the channel, even those destined for another host, and use tools such as tcpdump or wireshark to capture the traffic.

2. data link layer the data link layer ensures reliable communication between two directly connected devices. it organizes data into frames and provides error detection and correction mechanisms. this layer also controls access to the physical medium to prevent collisions when multiple devices attempt to transmit data simultaneously. protocols such as ethernet, ppp, and mac addressing mechanisms operate at this layer.

data link layer the data link layer ensures reliable communication between two directly connected devices. it organizes data into frames and performs error detection and correction. it also manages access to the physical medium using protocols such as ethernet.

gateways operate at multiple layers of the osi model, depending on the type of translation required, and they often perform complex tasks such as protocol conversion, data encapsulation, and message translation. in enterprise environments, gateways are critical for integrating legacy systems with modern networks, allowing organizations to maintain older infrastructure while adopting new technologies. they also play a role in connecting internal networks to external services, such as cloud platforms, by ensuring compatibility. because of their complexity, gateways are often more expensive and resource-intensive than routers or switches. however, their ability to bridge communication gaps makes them indispensable in diverse networking environments where multiple standards coexist.

an ethernet switch operates at the data link layer (layer 2) of the osi model to create a separate collision domain for each switch port. each device connected to a switch port can transfer data to any of the other ports at any time and the transmissions will not interfere. because broadcasts are still being forwarded to all connected devices by the switch, the newly formed network segment continues to be a broadcast domain. switches may also operate at higher layers of the osi model, including the network layer and above. a switch that also operates at these higher layers is known as a multilayer switch.

the file transfer protocol is designed for transferring files between computers connected through a network. ftp operates using a client-server architecture where users authenticate themselves to access remote servers. the protocol uses two separate communication channels: one for sending commands and another for transferring data. through ftp, users can upload files to servers, download files to local systems, and manage directory structures. although ftp was widely used during the early development of the internet, it does not provide built-in encryption. for this reason, secure variants such as ftps and sftp are often preferred in modern network environments.

the transport layer provides communication services that allow applications to exchange data over a network. tcp is widely used because it provides reliable and ordered delivery of data. it establishes a connection before data transmission and ensures that lost packets are retransmitted. udp is a simpler protocol that does not establish a connection and does not guarantee reliability. however, it is efficient for applications that require low latency. flow control mechanisms regulate the rate of data transmission to ensure efficient communication. congestion control algorithms detect congestion in the network and adjust the sending rate accordingly. port numbers allow multiple applications to communicate over the network simultaneously without interference.

hubs are physical hardware used to connect multiple devices operating on the same lan. by plugging laptops, desktops, printers and other devices into a hub port with an ethernet cable, higher speeds and more stable internet connections can be achieved.

in networking literature, the data link layer is responsible for providing reliable data transfer over a single link. framing converts the continuous bit stream from the physical layer into manageable units called frames. these frames include control fields that assist with synchronization and addressing. error detection and correction play a vital role because physical transmission is often susceptible to noise. techniques like checksums and crc help ensure data integrity. flow control is implemented to coordinate transmission rates between sender and receiver, preventing buffer overflow. mac addressing assigns unique hardware identifiers to network interface cards. through switching concepts, devices such as ethernet switches maintain forwarding tables that allow efficient frame delivery within local networks.

a network is a set of devices (often referred to as nodes) connected by communication links. a node can be a computer, printer, or any other device capable of sending and/or receiving data generated by other nodes on the network. �computer network�� to mean a collection of autonomous computers interconnected by a single technology. two computers are said to be interconnected if they are able to exchange information. the connection need not be via a copper wire; fiber optics, microwaves, infrared, and communication satellites can also be used. networks come in many sizes, shapes and forms, as we will see later. they are usually connected together to make larger networks, with the internet being the most well-known example of a network of networks. there is considerable confusion in the literature between a computer network and a distributed system. the key distinction is that in a distributed system, a collection of independent computers appears to its users as a single coherent system. usually, it has a single model or paradigm that it presents to the users. often a layer of software on top of the operating system, called middleware, is responsible for implementing this model. a well-known example of a distributed system is the world wide web. it runs on top of the internet and presents a model in which everything looks like a document.
presentation layer � concerned with how data is presented to the network � handles three primary tasks: � translation , �compression , �encryption.

the data link layer ensures that data frames are delivered accurately across network connections. framing defines frame boundaries and organizes transmitted bits. error detection and correction techniques verify frame integrity. flow control prevents receiver overload. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices in the network. switching concepts allow switches to forward frames effectively.

flow control is a mechanism that ensures the sender does not transmit data faster than the receiver can process it. in the context of transmission control protocol, flow control is implemented using the sliding window technique. this method allows the receiver to advertise the amount of buffer space available for incoming data. the sender then adjusts its transmission rate according to this information. if the receiver�s buffer becomes full, the receiver can temporarily stop the sender from sending additional data. once buffer space becomes available again, the sender resumes transmission. this dynamic adjustment helps maintain stable communication and prevents data loss caused by buffer overflow.

authentication this service assures that a communication is authentic. for a single message transmission, its function is to assure the recipient that the message is from intended source. for an ongoing interaction two aspects are involved. first, during connection initiation the service assures the authenticity of both parties. second, the connection between the two hosts is not interfered allowing a third party to masquerade as one of the two parties. two specific authentication services defines in x.80.

a gateway serves as a connection between networks, translating data to enable communication between different networks. historically, gateways and routers have been separate devices, but it�s becoming more common for them to be combined into a single router. for example, home wi-fi routers can act as both a router and a gateway, delivering data within the network while translating it for devices on the receiving end. a network gateway typically consists of physical components, such as network interface cards and inputs and outputs, as well as software for translating network protocols and providing gateway functions. these functions can be defined, deployed, and controlled through software, and may also be built into other devices, such as routers. gateways can be deployed on various layers of the open systems interconnection (osi) model, but are typically used on the network layer. they can be used in various security processes, including as a firewall or proxy server to scan and filter data, and can be used in either a unidirectional or bidirectional manner, allowing data to flow in only one direction or in both directions.

the ipv4 header contains essential fields needed for routing and control. it includes version, header length, type of service/dscp, total length, and identification/flags/fragment offset used for fragmentation and reassembly. other fields include time to live (ttl) to prevent loops, protocol to indicate the encapsulated higher layer, and header checksum for error detection. these fields collectively allow routers to forward packets correctly, manage fragmentation, support quality?of?service cues, and help in diagnosing problems such as ttl expiration.

the browser is displaying a web page on the client machine. each page is fetched by sending a request to one or more servers, which respond with the contents of the page. the request-response protocol for fetching pages is a simple text-based protocol that runs over tcp, just as was the case for smtp. it is called http (hypertext transfer protocol). the secure version of this protocol, which is now the predominant mode of retrieving content on the web today, is called https (secure hypertext transfer protocol). the content may simply be a document that is read off a disk, or the result of a database query and program execution. the page is a static page if it is a document that is the same every time it is displayed. in contrast, if it was generated on demand by a program or contains a program it is a dynamic page.

ring topology in a ring topology, it forms a ring connecting devices with exactly two neighboring devices. a number of repeaters are used for ring topology with a large number of nodes, because if someone wants to send some data to the last node in the ring topology with 100 nodes, then the data will have to pass through 99 nodes to reach the 100th node. hence to prevent data loss repeaters are used in the network. in-ring topology, the token ring passing protocol is used by the workstations to transmit the data where, token passing is a network access method in which a token is passed from one node to another node & token is a frame that circulates around the network.

anycast addressing allows multiple devices to share a single ip address, with packets routed to the nearest one according to routing protocols. widely used in content distribution and dns services, anycast improves load distribution and redundancy. unlike unicast (unique host) or multicast (group delivery), anycast focuses on reaching the �closest� host among many.

cloud security is a shared responsibility. in general, the public cloud infrastructure is secured by the service provider�such as aws, azure, or gcp�and the workloads running on the infrastructure are secured by the customer. to meet the business imperative of cloud migration, organizations need a network security solution in the cloud that offers advanced protection, flexibility, and predictable costs.

categories of networks networks are categorized on the basis of their size and geographical reach. the three basic categories are: a. local area network (lan) a lan is usually limited to a few kilometers. it may be privately owned and could be a network inside an office on one of the floors of a building, or it could consist of all the computers in an entire building. b. metropolitan area network (man) a man is intermediate in size between a lan and a wan. it is larger than a lan but smaller than a wan. it may comprise the entire network in a city. c. wide area network (wan) wan is made of all the networks in a geographically large area. it provides long-distance transmission of data, image, audio, and video information over large areas.

the data link layer ensures that frames are transmitted accurately across network links. framing structures transmitted bits into frames with control information. error detection and correction techniques maintain data integrity. flow control regulates communication speed between devices. mac addressing uniquely identifies network interfaces. switching concepts enable switches to deliver frames efficiently.

hybrid network combination of private and public networks some parts of the network are restricted, while others are publicly accessible allows organizations to maintain internal security while providing external connectivity common in modern enterprise and cloud-based systems

network security refers to the technologies, policies, people, and procedures that defend any communication infrastructure from cyberattacks, unauthorized access, and data loss, while upholding the principles of the cia triad. in addition to the network itself, they also secure traffic and networkaccessible assets at both the network edge and inside the perimeter.

tcp/ip model the tcp/ip model is the networking architecture used in the internet. it organizes communication functions into four layers. network interface layer this layer handles communication between devices connected to the same network. it includes functions related to hardware addressing and physical transmission.

the session layer creates the setup, controls the connections, and ends the teardown, between two or more computers, which is called a "session". common functions of the session layer include user logon (establishment) and user logoff (termination) functions. including this matter, authentication methods are also built into most client software, such as ftp client and nfs client for microsoft networks. therefore, the session layer establishes, manages and terminates the connections between the local and remote applications. the session layer also provides for full-duplex, half-duplex, or simplex operation,[citation needed] and establishes procedures for checkpointing, suspending, restarting, and terminating a session between two related streams of data, such as an audio and a video stream in a web-conferencing application. therefore, the session layer is commonly implemented explicitly in application environments that use remote procedure calls.

the network layer ensures packet transmission between networks using routing and logical addressing. devices are assigned addresses through internet protocol version 4 and internet protocol version 6. ipv6 supports a larger address space and better routing capabilities. subnetting divides networks into smaller subnetworks that improve performance and management. routing algorithms compute optimal routes based on link costs and topology information. routing protocols such as routing information protocol and open shortest path first allow routers to exchange routing tables. global routing across autonomous systems is performed using border gateway protocol.

dns over https (doh) is a protocol for performing remote domain name system (dns) resolution via the https protocol. a goal of the method is to increase user privacy and security by preventing eavesdropping and manipulation of dns data by man-in-the-middle attacks by using the https protocol to encrypt the data between the doh client and the doh-based dns resolver. by march 2018, google and the mozilla foundation had started testing versions of dns over https. in february 2020, firefox switched to dns over https by default for users in the united states. in may 2020, chrome switched to dns over https by default.

another important model is the tcp/ip model, which is widely used on the internet. it has four layers: the network interface layer, internet layer, transport layer, and application layer. these layers perform functions similar to those of the osi model but are grouped differently. the osi model is mainly used as a theoretical reference for understanding networking concepts, while the tcp/ip model is the practical architecture used in real-world internet communication.

4. transport layer this layer manages the communication between specific processes on the host computers. function: establishes end-to-end connection characteristics. focus: * sequencing: ensuring packets arrive in the correct order. retransmissions: resending data that was lost during transit. congestion control: preventing the network from being overwhelmed by too much data.

large-scale internet routing involves managing the routing of packets across thousands of autonomous systems worldwide. routers exchange routing information using the border gateway protocol (bgp) to determine how data should travel between networks. each router maintains a routing table containing information about reachable networks and preferred paths. as the internet continues to grow, routing scalability becomes increasingly important. techniques such as route aggregation, hierarchical addressing, and policy-based routing help maintain efficient routing operations. effective large-scale routing ensures that data can travel reliably across continents while minimizing delays and preventing routing loops.

a switch is a multi-port device designed to transmit incoming data packets to correct ports or devices based on the destination mac or hardware address. it�s typically more intelligent than a hub as it can improve the network performance with advanced features such as intelligent packet forwarding and full-duplex communication. it can also control access to various parts of the network, thereby enhancing network security. multi-layer switches can operate as a router and thereby known as advanced network devices.

types of computer networks computer networks can be classified based on geographical coverage. a local area network (lan) connects devices within a limited geographical area such as a home, office, or university building. lans usually provide high-speed communication and are privately managed. a metropolitan area network (man) covers a larger area such as a city. it connects multiple lans together and is often used by organizations or service providers. a wide area network (wan) spans very large geographic distances, sometimes connecting networks across different countries or continents. the internet is the largest wan and connects millions of networks worldwide.

media gateways: a media gateway takes care of the conversion process of data from one format to another, based on differences between networks. iot gateways: these aggregate sensor data from any devices that are connected to an iot environment. they translate data that flows between sensor protocols. before the data can move forward ,the iot gateway must translate and process it.

switches are more sophisticated than hubs, which are generally considered to be the simplest of network connecting devices. with both switches and hubs, each computer is connected through a single line, but switches are more intelligent about where it sends the data that comes through its ports. it does this by reading the hardware addresses of incoming packets and basing forwarding decisions on guidelines that are provided in the headers of the tcp/ip protocols. while switches and hubs perform a similar function, switches are often preferred because they improve network efficiency and network security. there are also multilayer switches, which function as both a switch and a router. a bridge is a networking device that filters and forwards packets based on a physical address. bridges are used to connect two or more network segments together and store and forward frames between the different segments. they are also used to divide more extensive networks into smaller sections and manage the flow of data between the sections.

3. internet layer also known as the network layer, this layer deals with packets and connects independent networks to transport data across boundaries. ip (internet protocol): handles addressing and routing of packets. icmp (internet control message protocol): used for error reporting and diagnostics

ospf supports hierarchical routing using areas to limit routing update scope and reduce overhead. the backbone area (area 0) connects all other areas, and routers internal to an area share full topology information. area design improves scalability by restricting frequent updates to local areas and only summaries between areas. ospf tractably manages large enterprise networks with multilevel topology.

the transport layer ensures reliable communication between applications by managing data transfer processes. tcp is designed to provide dependable communication by ensuring that data is delivered without errors and in the correct order. it includes mechanisms for flow control and congestion control to maintain efficient network performance. udp, in contrast, provides a connectionless service with minimal overhead. while it does not guarantee reliability, it offers faster transmission and is suitable for time-sensitive applications. flow control prevents the sender from overwhelming the receiver with excessive data. congestion control techniques adjust transmission rates during periods of heavy network traffic. port numbers help identify the destination application for incoming data.

network layers in most of the networks today, we do not follow the osi model of seven layers. what is actually implemented is as follows. the functionality of application layer and presentation layer is merged into one and is called as the application layer. functionalities of session layer is not implemented in most networks today. also, the data link layer is split theoretically into mac (medium access control) layer and llc (link layer control). but again in practice, the llc layer is not implemented by most networks. so as of today, the network architecture is of 5 layers only.

there are different types of network switches available, managed or unmanaged, smart switches, layer 2 or 3, power over ethernet switches, modular, fixed and stackable switches. when designing enterprise networks, infrastructure architects at seacom business ensure that the correct network switches are used at each part of the network.

network topology defines device arrangement and data flow: bus topology: single backbone cable; simple but limited scalability. star topology: devices connected to a central hub; easy management, reliable. ring topology: circular connection; data travels in one direction. mesh topology: devices interconnected; high reliability. tree topology: hierarchical combination of star and bus networks. protocols are standardized rules for communication, ensuring correct data transfer. examples include tcp (reliable transmission), ip (logical addressing and routing), http (web communication), ftp (file transfer), and dns (domain name resolution).

a wireless access point (wap) is a crucial networking device that enables wireless-capable devices to connect to a wired network. unlike traditional wired connections, waps are simple and easy to install, making it a convenient option for connecting all computers and devices in your network. there are several reasons to use a wap to set up a wireless network. for instance, it allows you to create a wireless network within your existing wired network, accommodating wireless devices and enhancing your overall network capability. additionally, waps or mesh extenders can be used to extend the signal range and strength of your wireless network, effectively eliminating �dead spots� and providing complete wireless coverage, which is particularly beneficial in larger office spaces or buildings. furthermore, waps can be configured using a single device, making it a seamless and efficient process.

protocols operating at the application layer include hypertext transfer protocol (http) for web browsing, file transfer protocol (ftp) for file transfers, simple mail transfer protocol (smtp) for email services, and domain name system (dns) for resolving domain names to ip addresses. these protocols ensure that user applications can effectively communicate with each other and with servers over a network.

networking systems depend on the data link layer for link-level reliability. framing structures transmitted bits into frames. error detection and correction mechanisms verify frame integrity. flow control manages the rate of communication between devices. mac addressing uniquely identifies network interfaces. switching concepts allow switches to forward frames to the appropriate destination ports.

in computer networks, the transport layer is responsible for providing communication services directly to application processes. two main protocols used at this layer are transmission control protocol and user datagram protocol. tcp is a connection-oriented protocol, meaning that it establishes a connection between sender and receiver before transmitting data. it ensures reliable delivery through acknowledgments, sequence numbers, and retransmission of lost packets. tcp is widely used in applications such as http, https, and ftp where reliable communication is required.

transition mechanisms like softwire protocols and 6to4 tunneling allow ipv6 packets to traverse ipv4 infrastructure by encapsulating ipv6 inside ipv4 packets. softwire supports automated protocols for ipv4/ipv6 coexistence, aiding the migration from ipv4 to ipv6 without native support on all segments. these techniques preserve connectivity while networks upgrade to ipv6.

within networking systems, the data link layer organizes communication between devices connected to the same physical medium. framing defines frame boundaries and adds control information. error detection and correction mechanisms verify data integrity and ensure reliable communication. flow control manages the rate of frame transmission. mac addressing uniquely identifies network devices. switching concepts allow switches to route frames efficiently using mac address tables.

guided media guided media, which are those that provide a conduit from one device to another, include twisted-pair cable, coaxial cable, and fiber-optic cable. a signal traveling along any of these media is directed and contained by the physical limits of the medium. twisted-pair and coaxial cable use metallic (copper) conductors that accept and transport signals in the form of electric current. optical fiber is a cable that accepts and transports signals in the form of light. 1. twisted-pair cable a twisted pair consists of two conductors (normally copper), each with its own plastic insulation, twisted together, as shown below figure. one of the wires is used to carry signals to the receiver, and the other is used only as a ground reference. the receiver uses the difference between the two. in addition to the signal sent by the sender on one of the wires, interference (noise) and crosstalk may affect both wires and create unwanted signals. if the two wires are parallel, the effect of these unwanted signals is not the same in both wires because they are at different locations relative to the noise or crosstalk sources (e,g., one is closer and the other is farther). this results in a difference at the receiver. by twisting the pairs, a balance is maintained. for example, suppose in one twist, one wire is closer to the noise source and the other is farther; in the next twist, the reverse is true. twisting makes it probable that both wires are equally affected by external influences (noise or crosstalk). this means that the receiver, which calculates the difference between the two, receives no unwanted signals. the unwanted signals are mostly canceled out. from the above discussion, it is clear that the number of twists per unit of length (e.g., inch) has some effect on the quality of the cable.

mesh topology in a mesh topology, every device is connected to another device via a particular channel. every device is connected to another via dedicated channels. these channels are known as links. in mesh topology, the protocols used are ahcp (ad hoc configuration protocols), dhcp (dynamic host configuration protocol), etc.

regulatory compliance & risk management effective network security ensures that organizations remain compliant with regulatory frameworks like the general data protection regulation (gdpr) and the payment card industry data security standard (pci dss). this reduces the risk of legal fines and penalties. strengthening access control & authentication network security strengthens access control and authentication, ensuring only authorized employees can access sensitive data and minimizing the risk of insider threats.

the network layer ensures packets are delivered from the source network to the destination network across multiple intermediate routers. logical addressing is performed using internet protocol version 4 and internet protocol version 6. ipv6 significantly expands the available address space and supports advanced networking features. subnetting is an essential technique that allows large networks to be divided into smaller subnetworks, improving efficiency and control over network traffic. routing algorithms determine optimal packet paths using information about network topology and link metrics. routing protocols such as routing information protocol and open shortest path first help routers exchange routing updates. for communication between different autonomous systems on the internet, routers rely on border gateway protocol.

network topologies network topology refers to the physical or logical arrangement of devices in a network. it determines how devices are connected and how data flows between them. common topologies include bus topology, where all devices share a single backbone cable. star topology, where each device connects to a central hub or switch. ring topology, where devices are connected in a circular loop and data travels around the ring. mesh topology, where every device connects to multiple other devices, providing redundancy and reliability. tree topology combines characteristics of star and bus networks to form hierarchical structures. each topology has advantages and disadvantages related to cost, scalability, reliability, and performance.

the data link layer is responsible for establishing reliable communication between devices connected to the same physical medium. one of its fundamental functions is framing, where the raw stream of bits received from the physical layer is divided into frames. these frames contain headers and trailers that include addressing and error-checking information. error detection and correction are implemented using techniques such as parity bits or cyclic redundancy checks to ensure that corrupted frames can be identified and handled properly. flow control regulates the rate of transmission so that the sender does not overwhelm the receiver. mac addressing allows each network interface card to have a unique hardware address, enabling accurate delivery. switching concepts allow switches to forward frames efficiently using mac address tables.

access points are used to create a wireless local area network (lan). these devices send and receive data so that visitors, clients and employees can wirelessly connect to the internet. internet signals are transmitted from the ap device using 2.4ghz and/or 5ghz bands. aps receive the signal via an ethernet cable, which is connected to the router.

congestion control is another essential function of the transport layer. it is designed to prevent excessive network traffic from overwhelming routers and switches. in transmission control protocol, congestion control is implemented through algorithms such as slow start and congestion avoidance. during the slow start phase, tcp gradually increases the amount of data it sends until it detects packet loss. packet loss is interpreted as a signal that congestion may be occurring in the network. when this happens, tcp reduces its transmission rate to relieve pressure on the network. these mechanisms help maintain efficient use of network resources and prevent network collapse during periods of heavy traffic.

tftp is a more basic version of ftp and works similarly but provides fewer services. using nfs, we can share files remotely over the internet. lpd helps in printing jobs between unix systems and remote servers. x window defines a protocol for writing gui applications. the smtp protocol builds a communication network between two email servers.

the process of looking up a name and finding an address is called name resolution. when a resolver has a query about a domain name, it passes the query to a local name server. if the domain being sought falls under the jurisdiction of the name server, such as top.cs.vu.nl falling under cs.vu.nl, it returns the authoritative resource records. an authoritative record is one that comes from the authority that manages the record and is thus always correct. authoritative records are in contrast to cached records, which may be out of date.

osi model the open systems interconnection (osi) model is a conceptual framework developed by the international organization for standardization (iso) to standardize communication between different computer systems. in a network environment, many complex operations are required for data communication, such as transmitting signals, detecting errors, controlling data flow, and providing services to applications. to simplify these tasks, the osi model divides the entire communication process into seven distinct layers, where each layer performs a specific function and communicates with the layers directly above and below it. the seven layers of the osi model are physical, data link, network, transport, session, presentation, and application. the physical layer is responsible for transmitting raw bits over the communication medium. the data link layer ensures reliable data transfer between directly connected nodes and handles error detection. the network layer manages logical addressing and routing of data packets across networks. the transport layer provides end-toend communication and ensures reliable data delivery through error recovery and flow control. the session layer manages communication sessions between applications. the presentation layer handles data formatting, encryption, and compression, ensuring that data is presented in a usable format. finally, the application layer provides network services directly to user applications such as email, file transfer, and web browsing. the osi model helps designers and engineers understand how networking systems operate and interact. by separating communication tasks into layers, the model makes it easier to develop interoperable networking technologies and troubleshoot communication problems.

3. transport layer the transport layer provides end-to-end communication services between applications. it manages data segmentation, error recovery, and flow control. the two main protocols at this layer are: � tcp (transmission control protocol) � reliable communication � udp (user datagram protocol) � fast but connectionless communication

what is a vpn service? a vpn, which stands for virtual private network, establishes a digital connection between your computer and a remote server owned by a vpn provider, creating a point-to-point tunnel that encrypts your personal data, masks your ip address, and lets you sidestep website blocks and firewalls on the internet. this ensures that your online experiences are private, protected, and more secure.

integrity means that data cannot be modified without authorization. like confidentiality, it can be applied to a stream of messages, a single message or selected fields within a message. two types of integrity services are available. they are: connection-oriented integrity service: this service deals with a stream of messages, assures that messages are received as sent, with no duplication, insertion, modification, reordering or replays. destruction of data is also covered here. hence, it attends to both message stream modification and denial of service. connectionless-oriented integrity service: it deals with individual messages regardless of larger context, providing protection against message modification only. an integrity service can be applied with or without recovery. because it is related to active attacks, major concern will be detection rather than prevention.

segment routing (sr) is an advanced network-layer routing technique designed to simplify traffic engineering and improve scalability. instead of maintaining complex state information for each traffic flow, routers encode the desired path within the packet itself using a sequence of instructions called segments. each segment represents a specific node or function that the packet must traverse. segment routing can operate with both mpls and ipv6 infrastructures, making it flexible for modern service provider networks. it reduces the need for additional signaling protocols while allowing administrators to control traffic paths precisely. this improves network efficiency, enables fast rerouting during failures, and supports high-performance applications such as cloud services and video streaming.
network services: networks provide file sharing, print sharing, email, voip, video conferencing, and cloud services. the internet is the largest network enabling global communication.

the network layer enables communication between different networks by providing logical addressing and routing functionality. devices are identified through ip addresses assigned using internet protocol version 4 or the more advanced internet protocol version 6. ipv6 introduces a large address space and improved routing efficiency. subnetting is used to create smaller subnetworks from a large address block, improving network performance and management. routing algorithms calculate the shortest path between routers using metrics like bandwidth or cost. routing protocols such as routing information protocol operate using a distance-vector algorithm, while open shortest path first uses a link-state approach. the global internet relies on border gateway protocol to exchange routing information between autonomous systems.

the modem connects to a cable/wire from the outside, bringing in internet data (email, youtube videos, google search results, files) from all over the world. a different wire that goes from your modem to your computer delivers your internet requests and activity to your computer and monitor.

the data link layer continues to perform the same essential responsibilities across different networking technologies. these responsibilities include framing, where data from the network layer is encapsulated into frames with clearly defined boundaries so that receivers can interpret the information correctly. error detection and correction methods such as checksums and cyclic redundancy checks are implemented to verify that frames are transmitted accurately across the physical medium. flow control ensures that communication between sender and receiver remains balanced and prevents congestion or buffer overflow. mac addressing uniquely identifies each device connected to the network, allowing frames to reach their correct destination. switching concepts allow network switches to examine mac addresses, maintain forwarding tables, and efficiently deliver frames within local area networks.

campus area networks (cans) cans are created by linking the lans located in two or more buildings that are in close proximity to each other, as shown in figure 1.8. connections between the buildings can be made using cabling or wireless technologies. note: the term campus lan is also used to describe a can.

error detection is another important function of the transport layer. in transmission control protocol, a checksum is used to verify the integrity of transmitted data. the sender calculates a checksum value based on the contents of the segment and includes it in the tcp header. when the receiver receives the segment, it performs the same calculation and compares the result with the transmitted checksum. if the values do not match, the segment is considered corrupted and is discarded. the sender will then retransmit the lost or damaged segment. this process ensures reliable and accurate communication between network hosts.

metropolitan area networks (mans) mans are created by linking the networks located at two or more sites within a city. connections can be made using cabling or wireless technologies, with optical fiber cabling often used to link a customer�s buildings to a facility operated by a telecommunications provider.

cloud workload security signifies the security of data, applications, and all the infrastructure in cloud environments. it is a leading trend in next-gen network security because of the increasing usage of cloud applications. organizations are transitioning to the cloud and prioritizing visibility, risk mitigation, and integration with devops to ensure secure distributed workloads across containers, virtual machines, and serverless environments. cloud workload security helps them achieve the goal by providing real-time threat detection and continuous network security without impacting performance.

physical layer the physical layer is dependent on the implementation and type of physical media used. ieee defines detailed specifications for each lan implementation. for example, although there is only one mac sublayer for standard ethernet, there is a different physical layer specifications for each ethernet implementations.

network security combines policies and technologies to protect systems and digital assets from unauthorized access, misuse, and disruption. by using layered defenses rather than a single control, it ensures data integrity and reliable performance across increasingly complex, distributed networks.

network protocols network protocols are standardized rules that define how data is transmitted between devices in a network. protocols specify how data packets are formatted, addressed, transmitted, and received. examples of important networking protocols include transmission control protocol (tcp), which provides reliable communication by ensuring that data packets are delivered in the correct order. internet protocol (ip) provides logical addressing and routing functions. hypertext transfer protocol (http) enables communication between web browsers and web servers. file transfer protocol (ftp) is used to transfer files between computers, and domain name system (dns) translates domain names into ip addresses. protocols ensure that devices manufactured by different companies can communicate effectively within the same network environment.

unguided media, also known as wireless communication media, transmits signals through electromagnetic waves. examples include radio waves, microwaves, and infrared signals. wireless communication technologies such as wi-fi, satellite communication, and cellular networks use unguided transmission media. the physical layer also defines signal encoding techniques and synchronization methods that ensure accurate data transmission.

network security is based on three main components: protection, detection and response. protection refers to any proactive security measures that the organization takes to prevent cyberattacks or other nefarious activity. this may include tools such as a next-gen antivirus (ngav) or policies like privileged access management (pam). detection is defined as any capability that helps the organization analyze network traffic, identify threats and contain them. most often, this capability is delivered in the form of an advanced endpoint detection and response (edr) solution. an edr is an intrusion detection tool that uses advanced data analytics to record and store network activity and identify suspicious system behavior. most edr tools also provide contextual information and remediation suggestions to cybersecurity specialists. response refers to the organization�s ability to remediate a security event as quickly as possible. tools usually include a managed detection and response (mdr) system, which is a cybersecurity service that combines technology and human expertise to perform threat hunting, monitoring and response.

an internet of things (iot) gateway provides the bridge (protocol converter) between iot devices in the field, the cloud, and user equipment such as smartphones. the iot gateway provides a communication link between the field and the cloud, and may provide offline services and real-time control of devices in the field.

the data link layer is responsible for ensuring reliable data transfer between directly connected nodes. framing encapsulates network-layer packets into frames. error detection and correction techniques verify frame integrity during transmission. flow control helps maintain balanced communication speeds. mac addressing uniquely identifies network devices within a lan. switching concepts allow network switches to forward frames efficiently using mac address information.

data link layer this layer ensures reliable node-to-node communication. it organizes data into frames and performs error detection to ensure data integrity. network layer the network layer handles logical addressing and routing of packets across multiple networks. it determines the best path for data transmission from source to destination.

multiplexing and demultiplexing recall that a fundamental idea of packet switching is to multiplex multiple flows of data over a single physical link. this same idea applies up and down the protocol graph, not just to switching nodes. in figure 1.11, for example, we can think of rrp as implementing a logical communication channel, with messages from two different applications multiplexed over this channel at the source host and then demultiplexed back to the appropriate application at the destination host. practically speaking, this simply means that the header that rrp attaches to its messages contains an identifier that records the application to which the message belongs. we call this identifier rrp�s demultiplexing key, or demux key for short. at the source host, rrp includes the appropriate demux key in its header. when the message is delivered to rrp on the destination host, it strips its header, examines the demux key, and demultiplexes the message to the correct application. rrp is not unique in its support for multiplexing; nearly every protocol implements this mechanism. for example, hhp has its own demux key to determine which messages to pass up to rrp and which to pass up to msp. however, there is no uniform agreement among protocols�even those within a single network architecture�on exactly what constitutes a demux key. some protocols use an 8-bit field (meaning they can support only 256 high-level protocols), and others use 16- or 32-bit fields. also, some protocols have a single demultiplexing field in their header, while others have a pair of demultiplexing fields. in the former case, the same demux key is used on both sides of the communication, while in the latter case each side uses a different key to identify the high-level protocol (or application program) to which the message is to be delivered.

the physical layer is responsible for the transmission and reception of unstructured raw data between a device, such as a network interface controller, ethernet hub, or network switch, and a physical transmission medium. it converts the digital bits into electrical, radio, or optical signals (analogue signals). layer specifications define characteristics such as voltage levels, the timing of voltage changes, physical data rates, maximum transmission distances, modulation scheme, channel access method and physical connectors. this includes the layout of pins, voltages, line impedance, cable specifications, signal timing and frequency for wireless devices. bit rate control is done at the physical layer and may define transmission mode as simplex, half duplex, and full duplex. the components of a physical layer can be described in terms of the network topology. physical layer specifications are included in the specifications for the ubiquitous bluetooth, ethernet, and usb standards. an example of a less well-known physical layer specification would be for the can standard. the physical layer also specifies how encoding occurs over a physical signal, such as electrical voltage or a light pulse. for example, a 1 bit might be represented on a copper wire by the transition from a 0-volt to a 5-volt signal, whereas a 0 bit might be represented by the transition from a 5-volt to a 0-volt signal. as a result, common problems occurring at the physical layer are often related to the incorrect media termination, emi or noise scrambling, and nics and hubs that are misconfigured or do not work correctly.

network components are divided into hardware and software. hardware includes nics (network interface cards), switches, routers, hubs, and gateways. software includes network operating systems, protocols, and management software, which coordinate communication between devices.

ipv6 link?local addresses (starting with fe80::/10) are used for communication within a single network segment. they are essential for ndp operations, router discovery, and local neighbor communication without requiring global or unique addresses.

static routes are manually configured forwarding entries in a router. they don�t adapt to topology changes unless manually updated, making them suitable for simple or stable network segments. they are useful for securing and controlling specific paths where dynamic routing is unnecessary or undesirable.

across many networking textbooks, the data link layer performs several consistent responsibilities. the first is framing, where raw bit streams are divided into frames so that the receiver can identify boundaries of transmitted data. each frame typically includes addressing information and control fields. error detection and correction mechanisms such as checksums and cyclic redundancy checks ensure that corrupted frames can be detected and retransmitted. flow control regulates the rate of transmission so that communication remains balanced between sender and receiver. mac addressing uniquely identifies each device within a local network environment. finally, switching concepts allow network switches to examine mac addresses and forward frames efficiently across local networks.

the transport layer acts as a bridge between application programs and the network infrastructure. it enables process-to-process communication and ensures that data is delivered to the correct application. tcp provides reliable communication by establishing a connection between sender and receiver before data transmission begins. it uses sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmission mechanisms to maintain reliability. udp is a simpler protocol that does not require connection establishment and therefore offers faster transmission. flow control ensures that the sender does not transmit more data than the receiver can process. congestion control mechanisms help regulate traffic in the network and prevent performance degradation. port numbers allow the operating system to direct incoming data to the correct application.

a computer network is a collection of interconnected computing devices that communicate with one another to exchange information and share resources. these devices may include personal computers, servers, printers, smartphones, routers, and switches. the communication between devices occurs through communication links such as copper cables, fiber-optic cables, or wireless signals.

unguided media unguided media, also known as wireless or unbounded transmission media , uses electromagnetic waves to transmit data without any physical medium. signals propagate through free space such as air or vacuum. the main types of unguided media are radio waves, microwaves, and infrared waves.

another important concept is network topology, which describes the physical or logical arrangement of devices within the network. the most common topologies include bus topology, star topology, ring topology, mesh topology, and tree topology. each topology has its own advantages and disadvantages related to cost, reliability, scalability, and performance.
